what_it_does: This file is a noticeboard request item that tracks the R13 work to add a mind row to Suite._CORPUS_REGISTRIES so minds become first-class create/inspect targets via MCP, and explains the sequencing and accessor-pattern issues.
observations: It is an open request-type noticeboard item authored by ch-al7jdfdr.
It proposes adding a mind entry to Suite._CORPUS_REGISTRIES at suite.py:352.
It is marked IN-SCOPE for R13 but sequenced after commit 9256df9.
It states the compose-proof stands without this change and it is not required by R13's 7 verify bars.
It identifies an accessor-pattern mismatch: _CORPUS_REGISTRIES rows expect self.<registry_attr> instance attributes, while runtime/minds.py exposes a module-level singleton mind_registry().
It suggests wiring options: assign the singleton to self.mind_registry or add a small adapter, after reading the create_*/cognition_info consumer.
The consumer use-case is MCP create-a-mind / inspect-a-mind.
It references related noticeboard items board://item-3c324c27 and board://item-100216c3.
standouts: The core task is first-classness for mind:// via _CORPUS_REGISTRIES.
The sequencing rationale is explicitly about non-blocking dependency, not low value.
The main technical blocker is reconciling registry-row instance attributes with a module-level singleton.
It links the work to the deferred-first-classness pattern and the file-disjoint law.
conventions: Follows the noticeboard item YAML front-matter schema with id, address, type, state, title, author_session, timestamps, and history.
Uses board:// URIs for internal item references.
Uses code path/line references and commit hashes.
Frames deferral through dependency/pattern analysis rather than parking.
concerns: The wiring approach is not decided: it lists two options but does not pick one.
It depends on reading consumer code first, so the item is not yet fully analyzed.
It assumes minds/<id>.py files are addable today but does not describe how MCP create will write them.
notes: Open R13 request to wire the mind registry into Suite._CORPUS_REGISTRIES, blocked on reconciling a module-level singleton with the instance-attribute registry-row pattern.
questions: Which exact consumer functions read _CORPUS_REGISTRIES and what row shape do they expect?
How does mind_registry() differ from the existing registry instances in constructor and interface?
What concrete MCP behavior changes when mind:// becomes first-class?
How will the file-disjoint law referenced in board://item-100216c3 constrain the implementation?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to track adding mind first-classness to _CORPUS_REGISTRIES, and the file does exactly that as a noticeboard request, so it matches.
apparent_themes: Sequencing by dependency/blocking rather than perceived value.
Reconciling module-level singletons with instance-attribute registry rows.
intention_signals: It is trying to make minds first-class corpus objects with create/inspect MCP surfaces, while carefully sequencing the work after a core seam and resolving an accessor-pattern mismatch.
novelty: It explicitly calls out that the existing _CORPUS_REGISTRIES row format assumes an instance attribute, which conflicts with the module-level singleton design in runtime/minds.py, and frames the fix as either direct assignment or an adapter.
contribution: It captures a scoped, sequenced task and its technical rationale so a fused system can preserve the intent to add mind:// first-classness without losing the dependency on core seams and the need for consumer analysis.
summary_for_embedding: Noticeboard request item tracking the R13 task of adding a mind registry row to Suite._CORPUS_REGISTRIES to enable MCP create/inspect for minds, sequenced after core seams due to an accessor-pattern mismatch with the module-level mind_registry singleton.
intention_for_embedding: To record and communicate the planned work, sequencing, and technical constraint for making minds first-class corpus objects via the registry system.
